items.myth: High accuracy means the confusion matrix looks good for every class.
items.reality: Accuracy weights all examples equally. On imbalanced data, a model can achieve high accuracy with many FN on rare positives — inspect row margins for the positive class, not only (TP+TN)/N.
items.example: 98% accuracy with TP=2, FN=48 on 50 positives — excellent accuracy, catastrophic recall on the event you care about.

items.myth: TP + FP + TN + FN can exceed the number of examples.
items.reality: Each example falls into exactly one cell — the four counts always sum to N. Double-counting happens when someone adds row and column totals instead of reading cells.
items.example: N=100 → TP=10, FP=5, TN=80, FN=5 sums to 100; each patient appears once.

items.myth: False positive and false negative are interchangeable labels.
items.reality: FP is a negative example predicted positive (false alarm); FN is a positive example predicted negative (miss). They carry different business costs and map to different matrix cells.
items.example: Cancer screen: FN = missed cancer (high cost); FP = unnecessary biopsy (different cost) — not the same error.

items.myth: One confusion matrix summarizes a model regardless of threshold.
items.reality: The matrix is threshold-specific. The same probabilistic model yields different TP/FP/TN/FN when the cutoff moves — ROC-AUC exists precisely because one table is not enough for threshold-free comparison.
items.example: Threshold 0.9: TP=30, FN=70. Threshold 0.3: TP=85, FP=200 — same model, two different matrices.
